metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `sidecar.norm_id` is ALWAYS criterion-scoped (criteria are unconditionally included in its hash), so the grouping fix-unit key MUST be derived from `(location, claim-normalization-without-criteria)` — remove the misleading 'reuse norm_id' primary path and state definitively that a new criteria-free key function is needed.
evidence: Plan text: 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py where it already matches across criteria; where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization)'
sidecar.py:281: `basis = " ".join(tokens) + "|" + ",".join(sorted(finding.get("criteria", []) or []))` — criteria are unconditionally included, so norm_id is ALWAYS criterion-scoped
The 'reuse norm_id' branch is therefore unreachable; only the 'derive from (location, claim-normalization)' branch ever applies
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in sidecar.py where it already matches across criteria', but reading the implementation shows norm_id is UNCONDITIONALLY criterion-scoped — criteria are always baked into the hash (`basis = tokens + "|" + sorted_criteria`). Two findings with identical location and claim text but different criteria will always produce different norm_id values. The function can never 'match across criteria'. The plan hedges with 'where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization)' — but since norm_id is ALWAYS criterion-scoped, the reuse path described first is unreachable and misleading. An implementer reading the plan could attempt to call norm_id as the grouping key and find it never groups multi-criterion findings.
findings.location: What section / 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py where it already matches across criteria'
scenarios: Implementer calls sidecar.norm_id(finding) as the grouping key; since criteria differ across co-criterion findings, every finding in the group gets a unique norm_id, and no grouping occurs — the bug the plan was designed to fix remains.
A future change to norm_id that drops criteria from the hash would accidentally enable grouping in unexpected contexts.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the grouping-key description with: 'Derive the fix-unit key as `(normalized_location, claim_token_set)` where claim_token_set reuses sidecar._NORM_STOP_TOKENS filtering on the finding text but EXCLUDES criteria from the hash — this is a new helper alongside norm_id, not norm_id itself (norm_id is unconditionally criterion-scoped and cannot serve as a cross-criterion grouping key).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the grouping key: since `sidecar.norm_id` is criterion-scoped (criteria baked into its hash), specify whether the plan introduces a new `fix_unit_key(finding)` function (criteria-stripped variant of `norm_id`) in `sidecar.py` or uses a (location, claim-normalization) tuple directly — and name that symbol/approach explicitly in the plan.
evidence: sidecar.py:281: `basis = " ".join(tokens) + "|" + ",".join(sorted(finding.get("criteria", []) or []))` — criteria are baked into the hash

The plan acknowledges the fork but says 'the implementation must state which in an inline design note' — this defers the decision to implementation without specifying which branch is authoritative, leaving the edit target underspecified
findings.finding: `norm_id` in `sidecar.py` is criterion-SCOPED (its hash includes the sorted criteria list), so two findings for the same defect at the same location but citing different criteria will produce different `norm_id` values and cannot be grouped by `norm_id` equality. The plan proposes 'reuse the existing ... norm_id machinery' for the fix-unit key, then acknowledges the fallback to '(location, claim-normalization)', but does not commit to which path is actually taken. A new cross-criteria key (stripped of criteria from the hash basis) is needed, and that is a new symbol, not a reuse.
findings.location: What section: 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py ... where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization)'
scenarios: Implementer uses raw `norm_id` for grouping — cross-criteria findings never group because their `norm_id` values differ by construction (criteria suffix differs per criterion).
Implementer creates a new `fix_unit_key` function (criteria-stripped hash) — the plan should name this new symbol and its file so it is a known edit target.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new `fix_unit_key(finding)` helper in `sidecar.py` that computes the same token-set hash as `norm_id` but WITHOUT appending the criteria, and call that the grouping key. Name it in the plan as a new edit target in `sidecar.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `sidecar.build_payload` / `_slim()` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` to the plan's edit-target list, specifying that `_slim` must pass through the new `group_id` (and optionally `members[]`) field so the emitted sidecar satisfies the AC3 oracle.
evidence: AC3: 'unit test reads the emitted sidecar dict and asserts 5 block-decision findings and a shared group id'
sidecar.py:358-455: `build_payload` calls `_slim(f)` for each finding; `_slim` explicitly field-selects what is persisted — a new `group_id` field would be silently dropped unless added to `_slim`
The plan names `sidecar.py` only for `norm_id` reuse, not as a target for `build_payload`/`_slim` modification
findings.finding: `sidecar.build_payload` / its inner `_slim()` function in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` must be modified to persist `group_id` (and optionally `members[]`) on findings for AC3 ('unit test reads the emitted sidecar dict and asserts 5 block-decision findings and a shared group id'), but `build_payload`/`sidecar.py` is not listed as an edit target in the plan.
findings.location: Scope section (IN: 'sidecar-lossless') and AC3
scenarios: Grouping sets `group_id` on the verdict's blocking finding dicts, but `_slim()` does not include `group_id` — the sidecar event omits it and the AC3 test assertion on the emitted sidecar dict fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (via code inspection of `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`) whether `norm_id` is criterion-scoped or cross-criterion, and replace the two-branch hedge with a single specified fix-unit key derivation approach before implementation begins.
evidence: Plan quote: 'reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py where it already matches across criteria; where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization) - the implementation must state which in an inline design note.'
The hedge 'the implementation must state which' reveals the plan author has not verified the current scoping of norm_id; both branches are left open simultaneously.

If norm_id is indeed criterion-scoped (the typical design for per-criterion dedup keys), reuse is impossible and a new key must be invented — a different, potentially larger change than the plan implies.
findings.finding: The plan asserts it will 'reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in sidecar.py' for cross-criterion fix-unit grouping, but immediately hedges with 'where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization)' — deferring resolution of which path actually applies to an inline implementation note. This is an unverified internal capability assertion: the plan never confirms whether norm_id in sidecar.py is or is not criterion-scoped, leaving the core algorithmic path unresolved at plan time.
findings.location: What section — 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery … where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization) - the implementation must state which in an inline design note.'
scenarios: norm_id turns out to be criterion-scoped; implementer invents a new key without the plan's guidance, silently diverging from the 'reuse' intent and producing an untested code path.
norm_id is NOT criterion-scoped and is reused, but it has different normalization semantics than what cross-criterion grouping needs, causing false grouping or missed grouping.
findings.suggested_fix: Open sidecar.py, check norm_id's signature and call sites. If criterion-scoped: specify the fix-unit key as `hash(normalize(location) + normalize(core_claim))` (independent of criterion) and remove the reuse option. If cross-criterion: confirm reuse and delete the alternative branch. Either way, the plan should contain one authoritative sentence, not a fork deferred to an inline note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve and state the grouping key algorithm in the plan: confirm that a NEW `(location, claim-normalization)` key is used (NOT the criterion-scoped `norm_id`), and specify the claim-normalization function (e.g., same token-set approach as `norm_id` but omitting the criteria suffix).
evidence: sidecar.norm_id hashes `tokens + '|' + sorted_criteria` — confirmed in sidecar.py:281. Using it as the grouping key would NOT group findings that share a claim across different criteria, since criteria are part of the hash basis — the opposite of the plan's stated goal.
The plan presents both options but uses 'where … where …' conditional syntax without resolving the condition.
ABSENCE: the plan contains no inline design note resolving the choice.
findings.finding: The grouping key decision is explicitly deferred ('the implementation must state which in an inline design note') but the plan itself never resolves it. Two incompatible candidates are named — reuse criterion-scoped `norm_id` (which folds criteria into the hash, so two findings with different criteria but the same claim get different keys) vs derive a location+claim-only key (which ignores criteria, grouping across all criteria as intended) — and the plan picks neither. An implementer cannot produce a deterministic grouping without this choice.
findings.location: What section — 'Grouping key: reuse the existing reword-tolerant norm_id machinery in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py where it already matches across criteria; where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization) - the implementation must state which in an inline design note.'
scenarios: Implementer reuses criterion-scoped norm_id → two findings for the same defect but different criteria get different keys → no grouping occurs → the bug is not fixed.
Implementer derives a new location+claim key → correct behavior, but the plan provides no spec for the claim-normalization algorithm (token set? substring? threshold?).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ambiguous 'reuse norm_id … OR derive …' clause with: 'Derive the fix-unit key as `fix_unit_key(f) = sha256(norm_location(f.location) + "|") + norm_claim(f.finding))` where `norm_claim` applies the same token-set approach as `norm_id` but WITHOUT the criteria suffix — so findings sharing the same defect at the same location group regardless of which criterion surfaces them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the sidecar group-key field (e.g., `group_id`) and add it to the `_slim()` field-selection list in `sidecar.py` as a plan-specified schema change.
evidence: sidecar.py:375-419 — `_slim()` lists all persisted fields explicitly; `group_id` is not present.
ABSENCE: `group_id` does not appear in `build_payload`, `_slim`, or any plan section describing sidecar schema changes.

The What section mentions 'the sidecar retains all 5 findings with decision=block plus the group key (lossless for calibration replay)' but the group key field name and placement in _slim() are never stated.
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'a shared group id' in the sidecar, but no `group_id` (or equivalent) field is defined for the sidecar payload. The existing `_slim()` function in `sidecar.py` has a fixed field-selection list; `group_id` is absent from it. Without explicitly adding `group_id` to `_slim()` and `build_payload`, the AC3 oracle test would read `None` for every finding.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 — 'unit test reads the emitted sidecar dict and asserts 5 block-decision findings and a shared group id'
scenarios: Implementer adds group_id to the verdict finding dict but not to _slim() → sidecar contains no group_id → AC3 oracle fails.
Implementer adds group_id to _slim() but name differs from what the test asserts → AC3 fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"group_id": f.get("group_id")` to the `_slim()` dict in `sidecar.py:375-419` and name this field explicitly in the plan's What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion with a concrete proving command that exercises the CLI rendering path and asserts the '(+N co-criteria: ...)' suffix appears in the output for a grouped finding.
evidence: ABSENCE: no AC verifies the CLI output format
'CLI renders the primary with a...' — stated as a requirement but excluded from ACs 1–4
findings.finding: The CLI rendering feature ('(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)' suffix) is stated as a deliverable in the What section but has no acceptance criterion or proving command exercising it.
findings.location: What section, fourth bullet: 'CLI renders the primary with a "(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)" suffix'; AC section has no corresponding AC.
scenarios: An executor delivers grouping logic and sidecar losslessness (all ACs green) but ships a CLI that omits the co-criteria suffix entirely; all ACs pass and the completion verdict is signed, yet the user-facing rendering is broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] Running the review CLI against a synthetic verdict containing a 5-finding group prints exactly one primary block line containing the substring "(+4 co-criteria:" followed by the 4 folded criteria ids. Oracle: `python -m pytest tests/unit -k "cli_render or co_criteria_suffix" -x` green, or a snapshot test against captured CLI stdout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (with a CLI-output oracle, e.g. a unit/integration test that captures rendered output) covering the '(+N co-criteria: ...)' suffix on the primary blocking finding.
evidence: Plan 'What': 'CLI renders the primary with a "(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)" suffix.'
AC1 oracle: 'new unit test; python -m pytest tests/unit -k "dedup or fanout" -x green' — covers grouping logic only, not CLI output
AC2, AC3, AC4: none reference CLI rendering
ABSENCE: no AC asserts the suffix appears in CLI output or that a rendering test covers the new format
findings.finding: The CLI rendering deliverable — 'CLI renders the primary with a (+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...) suffix' — is described in the What section but has no corresponding acceptance criterion that verifies it. No test, oracle, or pass condition is defined for the CLI output change.
findings.location: What section (CLI rendering sentence) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC4
scenarios: Implementer adds grouping logic and sidecar changes, lint/tests pass (AC4), but never implements or tests the CLI suffix — the plan would be 'done' by AC4 with the CLI change missing.
A reviewer validating ACs cannot confirm the CLI output format is correct because no oracle covers it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'AC5: When a group of 3 blocking findings shares a location and claim, the CLI output for the primary finding contains the string "(+2 co-criteria:"; oracle: unit test that calls the CLI renderer on a synthetic grouped result and asserts the suffix is present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `members[]` schema on the primary finding — specify that each member entry is `{id, criteria, norm_id}` (or equivalent minimal shape) and add the corresponding field to the sidecar `_slim()` field list.
evidence: sidecar.py:358-455 — `build_payload` calls `_slim(f)` with an explicit field list. `members[]` is absent from this list.
ABSENCE: no field definition for members[] appears anywhere in the plan or the referenced files.
AC3 references 'a shared group id' but not members[], creating an inconsistency between the What section and the ACs.
findings.finding: The `members[]` field placed on the primary finding is never defined structurally: no field list, no type (list of finding ids? list of full finding dicts? list of {id, criteria} pairs?). The plan says 'folded findings listed on the primary as members[]' but provides no schema. The sidecar `_slim()` function in `sidecar.py` has an explicit field-selection list — adding a nested `members[]` with undefined structure leaves a structural gap.
findings.location: What section — 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group … with its co-criteria and the folded findings listed on the primary as members[]'
scenarios: Implementer stores full finding dicts in members[] → sidecar payload balloons unexpectedly; the _slim() contract is violated.
Implementer stores only finding ids → AC3's 'lossless for calibration replay' claim is not satisfied, since calibration needs the decision=block data which is on the folded finding, not the id.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the grouping step is best-effort: if it raises, the verdict is emitted ungrouped (original blocking[] unchanged) and a warning is logged, consistent with every other floor step at this seam.
evidence: __init__.py:524-534 — both _maybe_apply_rising_floor and _maybe_apply_completion_floor are wrapped in try/except Exception in their implementations.
ABSENCE: the plan specifies no fallback for a grouping failure — no 'if the grouping step raises, emit the ungrouped verdict unchanged' clause.
__init__.py:536-539 — sidecar.emit is already best-effort; the grouping step before it is not specified as best-effort.
findings.finding: No fallback/error handling is specified for the grouping step itself. Every other step inserted at the same seam in `__init__.py` (`_maybe_apply_rising_floor`, `_maybe_apply_completion_floor`) has explicit `except Exception` handling with best-effort degradation. The plan adds a new step at this seam but specifies no failure behavior — if the grouping raises, the entire review fails.
findings.location: What section — 'After verdict production and before the sidecar emit (the seam in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py where the rising/completion floors already run)'
scenarios: A bug in the grouping implementation (e.g., a KeyError on a malformed finding) raises at the seam → the entire review fails rather than degrading gracefully to ungrouped output.
An edge case (e.g., a finding with no location field) causes the normalizer to raise → the review is aborted.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the grouping call in `try/except Exception: logger.warning('blocking-dedup grouping failed; emitting ungrouped', exc_info=True)` in the plan's What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the grouping-key strategy in the plan itself (not deferred to an inline note): specify whether `norm_id` or `(location, claim-normalization)` is authoritative, and add an AC assertion that validates the chosen key's behavior on a location-collision negative case.
evidence: 'where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization) - the implementation must state which in an inline design note'
The plan's own AC does not assert which key strategy was used, so an implementer could choose either without failing any AC
findings.finding: The plan defers the critical grouping-key design decision ('reuse norm_id' vs. '(location, claim-normalization)') to an inline implementation note rather than resolving it in the plan, creating a risk of implementation drift from the stated intent.
findings.location: What section / 'the implementation must state which in an inline design note'
scenarios: An implementer uses norm_id (criterion-scoped) as the grouping key, which groups findings with the same norm_id across locations — over-grouping unrelated findings
The inline design note is omitted and the implementation silently uses a weaker normalization, causing AC 2 (different locations not grouped) to be flakily green

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide in the plan (not deferred to inline notes) whether the fix-unit key reuses the existing criterion-scoped norm_id or derives a new (location, claim-normalization) pair, and state the normalization algorithm or reference for 'claim-normalization'.
evidence: 'the implementation must state which in an inline design note' — the plan identifies two possible approaches but does not decide between them, deferring the real design choice to the executor
findings.finding: The grouping-key design choice is explicitly deferred to the executor: 'the implementation must state which in an inline design note' leaves the decision between reusing criterion-scoped norm_id versus deriving a new (location, claim-normalization) key unresolved in the plan itself.
findings.location: What section, second bullet: 'where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization) - the implementation must state which in an inline design note'
scenarios: An executor could implement either approach, producing inconsistent grouping behavior across implementations, and there is no stated criterion to validate which key was actually used.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the deferred note with a concrete decision, e.g.: 'The fix-unit key is (normalized_location, claim_fingerprint) where claim_fingerprint = the first 64 chars of the lowercased, whitespace-collapsed finding text after stripping criterion-label prefixes; this is independent of criterion-scoped norm_id.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `coverage.counts['blocking']` is updated post-grouping: either correct it to `len(grouped_blocking)` (mirroring the rising-floor counts correction pattern) or explicitly state counts are NOT adjusted and document the invariant.
evidence: __init__.py:114-118 — the rising floor explicitly corrects `counts['advisory_surfaced']` and `counts['dropped']` after structural changes to the advisory list.
ABSENCE: the plan contains no analogous counts-correction clause for blocking findings after grouping.
AC1 only checks that 'the verdict remains BLOCK' but not whether counts are consistent — the gap may not be caught by the specified tests.
findings.finding: The plan does not address whether `coverage.counts['blocking']` should be adjusted after grouping. Currently the counts in the verdict record the number of blocking findings before grouping. If N=5 findings are grouped to 1 primary, `counts['blocking']` will report 5 while `len(verdict['blocking'])` is 1 — an internal inconsistency that already caused a correctness fix when the rising floor was applied (see `__init__.py:116-117` where `counts` is explicitly corrected after flooring).
findings.location: What section — 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group'; also implicitly: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:116-117 (precedent for counts correction after structural change)
scenarios: Downstream consumers (the claim gate, the attestation manifest at manifest.py:196) read `counts['blocking']` and display 5 while only 1 finding is surfaced — misleading audit output.
Calibration tooling that joins `counts` with actual `blocking[]` length detects a mismatch and flags it as a data integrity error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all sidecar consumers (calibration replay, aggregators, etc.) and confirm — or enforce via schema versioning — that they tolerate the new `group_id` and `members[]` fields without breakage.
evidence: 'folded findings keep decision=block in the sidecar (calibration data lossless)'
Plan adds group_id and members[] to sidecar records but names no consumers of the sidecar format beyond 'calibration replay'
ABSENCE: no statement that existing sidecar readers are forward-compatible with new fields
findings.finding: The plan adds new fields (`group_id`, `members[]`) to sidecar finding records but does not enumerate downstream sidecar consumers or confirm they are tolerant of these new fields.
findings.location: What section / 'sidecar retains all 5 findings … plus the group key'
scenarios: A calibration replay tool deserializes sidecar records into a typed dataclass and raises on unexpected fields
A downstream aggregator counts block-decision findings per criterion and double-counts because it iterates members[] unexpectedly
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `sidecar_schema_version` bump and document that consumers must treat unknown fields as optional, OR add an AC asserting that existing sidecar-reading paths handle records with/without `group_id` gracefully.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the unverifiable 'epic/audit-enrichment touches decide.py' rationale with a verifiable pointer (ticket id, ADR number, or branch name), or restate the exclusion as a pure architectural preference ('grouping is a pipeline-layer concern, not a decision concern') without referencing an unlocatable epic.
evidence: Plan text: 'Keep the change out of src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py (epic/audit-enrichment touches it)'
Grep for 'epic/audit-enrichment' across src/rebar/llm: no matches found
Grep for 'audit-enrichment' across src/rebar/llm: no matches found
decide.py exists and is confirmed at src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py but contains no reference to any audit-enrichment epic
findings.finding: The scope exclusion 'Keep the change out of src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py (epic/audit-enrichment touches it)' rests on an unverifiable codebase assertion: 'epic/audit-enrichment' does not appear anywhere in the repository. This is a non-trivial exclusion — it suppresses a candidate implementation site — but the stated reason cannot be grounded.
findings.location: What section / 'Keep the change out of src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py (epic/audit-enrichment touches it)'
scenarios: The exclusion is a false premise (no such epic touches decide.py), and an implementer wondering whether to put grouping logic there has no grounded reason to avoid it.
If the epic does exist but is tracked outside the repo, the exclusion is externally-grounded — but the plan provides no verifiable pointer to it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` (`_render_plan_review_text`) to the plan's edit-target list as the CLI consumer that must be updated to render the `(+N co-criteria: ...)` suffix on grouped primary blocking findings.
evidence: src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:536-537: `for f in result.get('blocking', []): sys.stdout.write(f'  [BLOCK ...]  {f.get("finding", "")}\n')` — no co-criteria suffix today
File is outside `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` and is not mentioned anywhere in the plan's scope
findings.finding: `_render_plan_review_text` in `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` (line 524–537) is the CLI rendering function that iterates `result.get('blocking', [])` and formats each blocking finding; it is an external consumer (outside `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`) that must be updated to emit the '(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)' suffix the plan specifies, but it is not enumerated as an edit target.
findings.location: What section: 'CLI renders the primary with a "(+N co-criteria: E4, G1G2, ...)" suffix'
scenarios: Implementation adds grouping and `members[]` to the verdict but forgets `_llm_commands.py` — the CLI output never shows the co-criteria suffix despite the plan's AC and the 'What' spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default tiebreak rule for primary-finding selection (e.g., 'first occurrence in verdict order, breaking ties by criterion id lexicographic order') so the behavior is deterministic and the AC1 unit test can assert on a specific primary.
evidence: 'highest priority' — the plan does not define what priority means here (first-in-list, highest criterion weight, a dedicated priority field, etc.)
findings.finding: 'Highest priority' as the selection rule for the primary blocking finding within a group is undefined — no priority ordering or tiebreak rule is stated.
findings.location: What section, third bullet: 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group (highest priority)'
scenarios: Two blocking findings in the same group have identical apparent priority; the executor must invent a tiebreak rule, leading to non-deterministic or inconsistently documented behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Primary = the finding that appears first in the verdict list; ties broken by criterion id alphabetically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge the 'not separately rendered' behavioral change as a justified breaking change and add either a rollback path (e.g., a flag to disable grouping) or an expand-contract note describing how to recover if over-grouping occurs.
evidence: Plan 'What': 'folded findings keep decision=block in the sidecar (calibration data lossless) but are not separately rendered.'
Previously, N blocking findings each rendered as separate CLI output lines; after this change, N-1 are hidden from rendered output.
No section of the plan describes a rollback mechanism, a feature flag to restore full rendering, or an expand-contract approach.
ABSENCE: no rollback/back-out plan is mentioned anywhere in Why, What, Scope, or AC sections.
findings.finding: The plan suppresses rendering of N-1 previously-visible blocking findings ('folded findings are not separately rendered') — a behavioral breaking change to CLI output that could affect downstream consumers — but provides no rollback path, expand-contract sequence, or acknowledgement of how to revert if the grouping logic misfires.
findings.location: What section: 'folded findings keep decision=block in the sidecar … but are not separately rendered'
scenarios: If the claim-normalization heuristic over-groups (incorrectly folds distinct findings), the suppressed findings are invisible to the operator with no way to restore them without a code rollback.
A CI system or human operator scripting on the number of rendered blocking findings would silently see fewer lines than before, with no migration notice.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Rollback / back-out' note in the plan: e.g., 'If grouping misfires, set REBAR_BLOCK_GROUPING=0 (env flag checked in the pipeline layer) to restore flat rendering; this flag is removed once grouping is validated over N rounds.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md section or lightweight ADR capturing the fix-unit grouping stage: its position in the pipeline, the (location, claim-normalization) key derivation rationale, the members[]/group_id sidecar contract, and the CLI rendering convention.
evidence: Plan says 'the implementation must state which in an inline design note' — an inline code comment is not an architectural decision record
No mention of ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
Plan introduces a new pipeline stage, a new sidecar field (group_id), a new CLI rendering convention — all of which are durable architectural decisions future contributors need to discover
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline stage (fix-unit grouping layer in plan_review/__init__.py) and a new cross-cutting contract (the group_id field, the members[] structure, the '(+N co-criteria)' CLI rendering), but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture this architectural decision. The only documentation artifact is a vague 'inline design note' in the implementation itself.
findings.location: What section / Scope section / AC list — no doc/ADR artifact named
scenarios: A future reviewer adds a second grouping pass (e.g., for advisories) and doesn't know whether to follow the same (location, claim-normalization) key convention or reuse norm_id directly — no durable record exists
Calibration-replay consumers see group_id in the sidecar and must reverse-engineer its semantics from the inline comment rather than a spec
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'docs/adr/NNN-fix-unit-dedup.md' (or a CLAUDE.md section) with: (1) context — the multi-criterion fanout problem, (2) decision — group by (normalized_location, normalized_claim) after verdict production and before sidecar emit, (3) consequences — sidecar losslessness via decision=block on folded members, group_id for calibration replay. Reference the note from the inline design comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'highest priority' for primary-finding selection within a group (e.g., first-encountered, highest criterion rank, or explicit priority field) and specify a deterministic tie-breaking rule.
evidence: 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group (highest priority), with its co-criteria and the folded findings listed on the primary as members[]'
ABSENCE: no definition of 'priority' order (is it finding order? criterion rank? something else?) and no tie-breaking rule
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'highest priority' as the criterion for selecting the primary finding within a group but does not define what 'priority' means in this context or how ties are broken, leaving the implementation under-specified.
findings.location: What section / 'Surface ONE primary blocking finding per group (highest priority)'
scenarios: Two blocking findings in the same group have equal priority — implementation picks arbitrarily, making test outcomes non-deterministic
CLI suffix order of co-criteria changes between runs if tie-breaking is unstable, confusing users comparing runs
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Primary is the finding with the lowest criterion sort-key (alphabetical criterion id); ties broken by finding order in the verdict list. Co-criteria in the suffix are sorted alphabetically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the fix-unit key's claim-normalization COMPOSES with `sidecar.norm_id`'s token logic (e.g. call `norm_id` with an empty criteria list, or extract the token step as a shared helper) rather than re-implementing the regex/stop-token/sorted-set from scratch.
evidence: sidecar.norm_id basis: `" ".join(tokens) + "|" + ",".join(sorted(finding.get("criteria", []) or []))` — the criteria suffix is the only difference between norm_id and the needed fix-unit key
Plan text: 'where norm_id is criterion-scoped, derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization) — the implementation must state which in an inline design note'
The plan defers the choice to the implementer without directing them toward composition with norm_id's existing token logic
findings.finding: The plan proposes deriving a new '(location, claim-normalization)' fix-unit key rather than composing with the existing sidecar.norm_id() token-normalization logic. The token-extraction code in norm_id (re.findall([a-z0-9]+), stop-token filter, sorted-set) is exactly the 'claim-normalization' the plan needs — minus the criteria suffix. The plan leaves open whether to strip the criteria from norm_id or write a new function, with 'the implementation must state which in an inline design note.' Without explicit guidance, the implementer may re-write the token logic independently, creating two near-identical normalization paths in the same module.
findings.location: What section — 'derive the fix-unit key from (location, claim-normalization)'
scenarios: Implementer writes a standalone claim_key() that duplicates re.findall/stop-token/sorted-set from norm_id — two nearly identical normalization functions in sidecar.py with no shared root.
Future change to norm_id's stop-token threshold or regex doesn't propagate to the fix-unit key, causing grouping behavior to diverge silently from the norm_id-based recall matching.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a `_claim_tokens(finding)` helper that extracts the significant-token set (the `re.findall` + stop-token filter already in `norm_id`), then have `norm_id` call it and have the fix-unit key derivation call it too — one shared token-extraction root, two consumers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the parent epic (5fd2) to include the fix-unit grouping concept and explicitly reconcile the 'ALL blocking findings are returned' cap-exemption invariant with the new 'folded findings are not separately rendered but kept in the sidecar' behavior — OR rewrite the leaf to align its rendering guarantee with the parent's existing 'ALL blocking findings returned' language (e.g., describe grouping as a rendering optimization, not a filtering of surfaced findings, with the parent's blessing).
evidence: Parent epic 5fd2: 'The cap NEVER drops BLOCKING findings — blocking is EXEMPT; if blocking findings exceed N, ALL of them are returned (the cap can never weaken the block decision).'

Leaf plan: 'folded findings keep decision=block in the sidecar (calibration data lossless) but are not separately rendered' — i.e., N blocking findings for one fix-unit yield only 1 rendered finding, not N, diverging from the parent's 'ALL of them are returned' language.

ABSENCE: parent epic 5fd2 contains no mention of members[], group_key, fix-unit grouping, co-criteria suffix, or any mechanism to fold multiple blocking findings into a primary-with-members representation.
The leaf's 'What' section introduces the concepts of 'fix-unit key', 'primary blocking finding', 'co-criteria', and 'members[]' — all novel to the leaf, not inherited from the parent's plan.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces cross-criterion fix-unit grouping (members[], group_key, co-criteria CLI suffix, non-rendered folded findings) — a structural concept and pipeline step entirely absent from the parent epic 5fd2. Specifically, the parent explicitly guarantees 'if blocking findings exceed N, ALL of them are returned' (the cap-exemption invariant), while the leaf marks folded findings as 'not separately rendered' — a potential contradiction of that parent guarantee that the parent plan would need to sanction before the leaf can diverge.
findings.location: What / Scope / AC sections of the leaf plan; parent epic 5fd2 §'Unified priority score + advisory surfacing CAP' ('The cap NEVER drops BLOCKING findings — blocking is EXEMPT; if blocking findings exceed N, ALL of them are returned')
scenarios: An implementer following the parent's contract would expect ALL blocking findings to be rendered (parent guarantee). The leaf's folding-into-members changes this for multi-criterion co-citation cases, but without parent authorization the leaf is diverging from the parent's stated invariant.

A future agent reading the parent to understand the gate's blocking behavior would see 'ALL blocking findings are returned' and not anticipate that some may be folded into non-rendered members[] of a primary — the parent contract is incomplete for this new behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to parent epic 5fd2 (or its active child that owns the sidecar/rendering seam) clarifying that the 'ALL blocking findings returned' guarantee means all N retain decision=block in the sidecar, while the CLI/rendered surface may present N co-criterion findings as 1 primary + N-1 members for UX clarity — making the leaf a consistent narrowing rather than a divergence.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
